serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of garlic noodles is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at an Indian-Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hakka Noodles (stir-fried, with egg) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for garlic, butter, and wheat noodles for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Garlic Noodles' exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Hakka noodle data (stir-fried wheat noodles with egg and vegetables) adjusted for garlic-butter seasoning using USDA FoodData Central component data. The dish is served in three variants (veg, egg, chicken); the chicken variant adds approximately 3–5 g protein per 100 g. Values below represent the egg variant as the mid-point reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style garlic noodles. GI of 52 is taken from boiled wheat noodles (GI 52, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (University of Sydney GI Database). Stir-frying with garlic and butter does not substantially alter GI relative to the noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 76.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 39.8, rounded to 40.1 accounting for minor sugar contribution from sauces. gi_category set to 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving of garlic noodles provides approximately 1,260 mg sodium, exceeding 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and avoid adding extra salt or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low) but a glycemic load of ~40 per standard 300 g serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with protein (egg or chicken variant) to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and avoid high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and elevated sodium from soy-based seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The butter-based garlic sauce contributes approximately 7.2 g saturated fat per 300 g serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the vegetable variant, request less butter, and be mindful of the high sodium load which may exacerbate hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Noodles is a popular stir-fried noodle dish served at Indian-Chinese restaurants in Singapore, including at Dugout on Circular Road. The dish consists of wheat-based noodles (typically Hakka-style or thin egg noodles) tossed in a fragrant garlic-butter sauce enriched with soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onions. It is available in three variants: vegetarian (veg), with egg, and with chicken. The garlic is typically sautéed in butter until golden, imparting a rich, aromatic flavour with a slightly nutty finish. The noodles are soft with a light chew, coated in a savoury, umami-forward sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 555 kcal, 19.5 g protein (egg variant), 20.4 g fat, and 76.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1,260 mg per serving) due to soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is substantial (~40), so portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a staple of the Indian-Chinese culinary tradition, which blends South Asian spicing techniques with Chinese noodle preparations, and is widely popular across Singapore's hawker and casual dining scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic noodles?
Is garlic noodles good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of garlic noodles?
Can I eat garlic noodles if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between veg, egg, and chicken garlic noodles?
What type of noodles are used in garlic noodles?
Is garlic noodles an Indian or Chinese dish?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for garlic noodles exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Hakka noodle data and USDA FoodData Central component data for garlic, butter, and wheat noodles. GI is analogously estimated from boiled wheat noodles per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the egg variant as the mid-point reference across the three variants (veg, egg, chicken). The chicken variant adds approximately 3–5 g protein per 100 g; the veg variant reduces protein by approximately 2–3 g per 100 g and eliminates cholesterol contribution from egg. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source or USDA component data for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from boiled wheat noodles (Atkinson et al. 2021, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Indian-style garlic noodles was found in approved sources. 6. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as the primary macronutrient and caloric base of this dish is wheat noodles. 7. Halal flag set to true as the dish is served at an Indian restaurant context and pork/lard is not a standard ingredient; however, consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects minor trans fat contribution from butter; this is within normal range for dairy-derived fat.
